findings: The full-body bird has one head, one beak, two legs, one tail, and normal shorebird anatomy; one folded wing is visible while the opposite wing is naturally occluded by the profile view.
The separate wing and head illustrations read clearly as conventional detail studies rather than duplicated anatomy.

The robust body, long heavy straight bill, bluish-gray legs, gray-brown plumage, short tail, and diagnostic black-and-white flight-wing pattern agree with the reference photographs and live identification sources.
The common name, scientific name, and family are correct.
All displayed measurements exactly match Audubon's published ranges. Cornell gives a slightly broader alternative range but does not contradict the displayed values.
The habitat, feeding behavior, vocal behavior, and every listed field mark are supported by the reviewed sources.
The main bird's lightly mottled plumage is plausible for a Western breeding bird or transitional plumage, though it is less strongly barred than a typical Eastern breeding adult.
No place name, ZIP code, coordinates, map, range map, or local-observation detail appears.
No material species, anatomy, composition, or text error was found.

verification_sources.title: Willet Identification — All About Birds, Cornell Lab of Ornithology
